source_paper: Experts’ recommendations for the management of adult patients with cardiogenic shock
author: Nadia Aissaoui et al.
pearl: For patients with cardiogenic shock, utilize a multidisciplinary 'Shock Team' consisting of at least a heart failure specialist, an interventional cardiologist, and an intensivist or cardiac anesthesiologist to facilitate rapid SCAI staging and collaborative decision-making regarding temporary mechanical circulatory support (t-MCS).

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Staphylococcus aureus (especially MRSA) has surpassed viridans group streptococci as the most common and virulent pathogen in developed countries, causing rapid valvular destruction, perivalvular abscesses, and systemic embolization.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Under the 2023 Duke-ISCVID criteria, a definitive clinical diagnosis of infective endocarditis requires the presence of either 2 major criteria, 1 major and 3 minor criteria, or 5 minor criteria.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: To satisfy the major microbiological criterion, typical pathogens (such as S. aureus, S. lugdunensis, E. faecalis, VGS, and HACEK) must be isolated from at least 2 separate blood culture sets, whereas less common pathogens require isolation from at least 3 separate sets.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Major microbiological criteria for intracellular pathogens are met with a positive PCR for Coxiella burnetii, Bartonella spp., or Tropheryma whipplei, an IgG anti-phase I titer for C. burnetii > 1:800, or an IFA IgG titer >= 1:800 for B. henselae or B. quintana.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: When using 18F-FDG PET/CT to identify abnormal metabolic activity as a major imaging criterion, the prosthetic valve must have been implanted for at least 3 months to avoid false-positive results from physiological postoperative inflammation.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Glomerulonephritis qualifies as an immunological minor criterion for infective endocarditis and is clinically defined by acute kidney injury associated with hematuria, proteinuria, red cell casts, or hypocomplementemia.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: The historical goal-directed target for static CVP (8-12 mmHg or 12-15 mmHg in mechanically ventilated patients) has been abandoned by major guidelines due to lack of survival benefit in septic shock.

pearl: For HRS-AKI, co-administer albumin (20-40 g/day) with vasoconstrictors like terlipressin or norepinephrine.

author: Jonathan Soldera

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease
author: Jonathan Soldera

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease
author: Jonathan Soldera
pearl: In the management of severe ACLF, early enteral nutrition is highly recommended; avoid protein restriction even in HE patients.

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease
author: Jonathan Soldera

source_paper: Beyond the Tipping Point: Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure and End-Stage Liver Disease

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis
author: Maren L. Smith et al.
pearl: For recurrent overt HE, combine lactulose with rifaximin (550 mg BID) to target both ammonia generation and systemic neuroinflammation.

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

author: Maren L. Smith et al.

source_paper: Gut microbiome-brain-cirrhosis axis

author: Sanju Mathew et al.

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies

author: Sanju Mathew et al.
pearl: For Intermediate Stage (BCLC B) HCC, locoregional therapies like TACE, bland embolisation (TAE), or SIRT are indicated, exploiting the liver's dual blood supply (80% portal vein, 20% hepatic artery).

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
author: Sanju Mathew et al.
pearl: For Advanced Stage (BCLC C) HCC with compensated function, Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab or the STRIDE regimen are first-line systemic therapies; Atezolizumab/Bevacizumab showed a median OS of 19.2 months (IMbrave150).

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
author: Sanju Mathew et al.

source_paper: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
author: Sanju Mathew et al.
pearl: AFP is the only validated biomarker for surveillance but has a sensitivity of less than 50% when used alone and can be falsely elevated in pregnancy or active hepatitis.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Perform diagnostic ascitic tap immediately for any patient with ascites, without waiting for coagulation correction.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Administer parenteral thiamine (>= 72h if Wernicke's suspected; 24h if not) before glucose-containing fluids in alcohol-related liver disease.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For SBP, initiate renal protection with intravenous 20% albumin (1.5 g/kg at diagnosis, 1 g/kg at 72 hours).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: In AKI management, suspend all NSAIDs, diuretics, and beta-blockers; for HRS-AKI, consider Terlipressin (2-8 mg/day) plus 20% Albumin (100 mL BID).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For AVH, administer Terlipressin (2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg QDS) and discontinue it 24 hours post-endoscopy.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Maintain a restrictive transfusion strategy for AVH: transfuse PRBCs only if Hb < 70 g/dL (target 70-80 g/dL); avoid FFP/platelets to correct labs.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: A diagnostic paracentesis must be performed in all patients with ascites within the first 6 hours to rule out SBP, regardless of symptoms or inflammatory markers.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: SBP is diagnosed with ascitic fluid PMN count > 250/mm3.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Secondary prophylaxis for SBP should start immediately after acute antibiotic course completion (e.g., co-trimoxazole 960 mg daily).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Initial AKI management requires suspending diuretics, beta-blockers, and NSAIDs; crystalloid boluses of 250 mL can be used to target UO > 0.5 mL/kg/hr.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: HDU escalation for AKI is needed if there is persistent hypotension, UO < 0.5 mL/kg/hr, rising lactate, or respiratory failure after 6 hours.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For HRS-AKI, pharmacotherapy includes Terlipressin (2-8 mg daily, titrated) combined with 20% albumin (100 mL BID).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: All upper GI bleeds in cirrhotic patients should be treated as AVH until proven otherwise.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Pre-endoscopy pharmacotherapy for suspected AVH includes Terlipressin (2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg QDS) or Octreotide (50 mcg IV bolus, then 50 mcg/hr infusion if terlipressin is contraindicated).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Empirical IV antibiotics and IV erythromycin (250 mg) are recommended pre-endoscopy for AVH.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: HE management requires titrating lactulose to achieve 2-3 soft stools daily; Rifaximin is reserved for secondary prevention of recurrent/chronic HE and does not acutely improve active episodes.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For non-tense ascites or peripheral edema, diuretics are used (Spironolactone 100 mg daily + Furosemide 40 mg daily), titrated every 72 hours to target 0.5-1.0 kg daily weight loss.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Malnutrition requires a high-protein diet (1.5 g/kg/day) with small, frequent meals and aggressive replacement of potassium, magnesium, and phosphate.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: VTE prophylaxis with tinzaparin is required for all patients with a platelet count > 50 x 10^9/L in the absence of active bleeding.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Corticosteroid therapy (Prednisolone 40 mg daily) is indicated for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis (AH), determined by prognostic scores like Maddrey's Discriminant Function or MELD, after excluding active infection and GI bleeding.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: If a patient with severe AH is a non-responder (Lille score > 0.45) at Day 4-7, corticosteroid therapy should be stopped immediately.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For urgent liver transplant assessment in ARLD, consider patients < 60 years old with 3-organ failure, no contraindications, and at least 3 months of documented abstinence.

pearl: Implement routine FIB-4 screening for high-risk populations (T2D, obesity, metabolic syndrome), integrating it into annual diabetes reviews.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

author: Catherine Hsu et al.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Statins are safe and essential for primary/secondary prevention in MASLD patients due to the critical role of cardiovascular protection.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Restrict paracetamol use to 2–3 g/24h in stable cirrhosis; for acute end-of-life pain, subcutaneous morphine 2.5 mg hourly is appropriate, but substitute with fentanyl if eGFR is reduced.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: For patients discharged after hepatic encephalopathy (HE), a clear lactulose titration plan targeting 2–3 soft stools/day and consideration of rifaximin are essential components of care.

author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Implement the Bristol Screening Tool on acute medical admission: a score > 2 suggests high risk of death within 12 months and mandates immediate supportive/palliative referral and Advance Care Planning discussion while cognitive capacity is retained.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Early palliative care referral in HCC is associated with fewer inpatient bed days, lower rates of in-hospital death, and reduced healthcare costs compared to standard care.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

pearl: Sarcopenia prevalence correlates strongly with frailty and poor clinical outcomes; early dietician involvement for nutritional optimization improves QOL and reduces covert HE symptoms.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: In patients with concurrent renal impairment or hepatorenal syndrome, morphine must be avoided or replaced with fentanyl when managing pain due to risk of toxic metabolite accumulation.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: Corticosteroids and G/M-CSF did not demonstrate a survival benefit in sepsis according to this network meta-analysis.

author: Luo R, et al.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside

author: Fiona Howroyd et al.

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy
author: Amanda Hill et al.
pearl: Do not withhold life-saving radiological imaging or emergency medications due to fears of fetal harm; delaying diagnosis causes greater maternal and fetal harm.

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy
author: Amanda Hill et al.

source_paper: Acute confusion in pregnancy

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: For septic shock, initial management requires prompt administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics and fluid resuscitation within the first hour.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor of choice for septic shock due to its potent alpha and beta activity.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Goal-directed fluid resuscitation should aim for a rapid initial bolus of 30 mL/kg crystalloid, followed by reassessment based on dynamic measures (e.g., lactate clearance).

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Lactate levels must be measured frequently and tracked; persistent elevation (>2 mmol/L) suggests ongoing tissue hypoperfusion.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Source control (e.g., surgical debridement of necrotic tissue, removal of infected lines) is a critical component of septic shock management.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: In patients with suspected pneumonia/ARDS, low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg predicted body weight) and PEEP titration are standard initial strategies.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Permissive hypercapnia (allowing PaCO2 > 45 mmHg) is acceptable in the early stages of septic shock unless coagulopathy or severe acidosis dictates otherwise.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) may be preferred over intermittent hemodialysis for fluid and solute removal in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury due to better hemodynamic stability.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: The use of prophylactic antibiotics should be avoided unless there is a clear indication or high risk of infection.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: For septic shock, initial resuscitation requires a combination of crystalloid and vasopressors to maintain Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) ≥ 65 mmHg.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: The lactate clearance rate (LCR) is a valuable prognostic marker; sustained LCR ≥ 1.0 mmol/L/hr suggests improved tissue perfusion and guides ongoing resuscitation efforts.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Early goal-directed therapy (EGDT) should guide initial management in septic shock, focusing on achieving adequate lactate clearance and MAP targets rather than solely relying on sequential organ failure criteria.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: In patients with suspected sepsis/septic shock, obtain blood cultures and administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within the first hour of recognition to minimize mortality.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Source control (e.g., removal of infected hardware, abscess drainage) is a critical component of septic shock management and must be aggressively pursued early in the course.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: For mechanical ventilation in ARDS, prone positioning should be considered for all patients meeting specific criteria (e.g., low oxygenation index, high PEEP settings) to improve V/Q matching and reduce mortality.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: Permissive hypotension (target MAP 55-60 mmHg) is recommended in hemorrhagic shock until surgical control of bleeding is achieved, provided there are no signs of coagulopathy or severe acidosis.

source_paper: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
pearl: When managing acute kidney injury (AKI) in the critically ill patient, fluid status assessment must be individualized; avoid routine aggressive diuresis unless euvolemia and hyperkalemia/acidosis mandate it.

pearl: For patients with suspected septic encephalopathy, early management includes optimizing glucose levels (target 140-180 mg/dL) and maintaining adequate cerebral perfusion pressure.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: For de novo acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, HFNC is strongly recommended over standard oxygen to reduce the need for intubation.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: For acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, NIV via facemask is strongly recommended over standard oxygen to reduce mortality and intubation.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: HFNC is a conditional alternative to NIV for hypercapnic respiratory failure with mild acidemia (e.g., pH > 7.25), provided close monitoring and prompt escalation to NIV are available.
topic: acid-base balance, respiratory failure, high-flow nasal cannula

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: For preoxygenation before endotracheal intubation, HFNC or NIV via facemask is strongly recommended over standard oxygen to prevent peri-intubation hypoxemia.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: Post-extubation support (HFNC or NIV) is suggested for patients extubated in the ICU after critical illness; NIV via facemask is specifically suggested for high-risk patients (e.g., age >65 years, obesity, hypercapnia, prolonged mechanical ventilation).

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: In acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, HFNC was recommended over standard oxygen therapy.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: In acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, NIV via facemask was recommended over standard oxygen therapy.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: For preoxygenation in adults with acute respiratory failure undergoing endotracheal intubation, HFNC or NIV via facemask is recommended over standard oxygen therapy.

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials significantly reduce the incidence of early-onset VAP (EO-VAP) by 48% (RR, 0.52; 95% CI, 0.41–0.66).

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials reduce overall VAP incidence by 35% (RR, 0.65; 95% CI, 0.55–0.77).

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: Prophylactic use of systemic antibacterials does not significantly reduce in-hospital mortality or duration of mechanical ventilation.

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials show a significant VAP reduction (RR, 0.60; 95% CI, 0.49–0.75) specifically in brain injury patients, but not in cardiac arrest patients.

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: IV administration of systemic antibiotics is preferred over topical agents due to superior systemic distribution and ease of use via existing central venous access.

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: The potential mortality benefit observed in combined (systemic + topical) antibacterial studies may require the synergistic effect of both components.

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: Antibacterial agents should be administered within 24 hours of mechanical ventilation initiation to maximize prophylactic efficacy.

source_paper: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Qiuying Yu et al.
pearl: The cost savings associated with a 35% VAP reduction are substantial, estimated at $9,969 per case in the U.S. and ~£10,000 in the U.K..

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: Lung ultrasound (LUS) significantly outperforms chest X-ray (CXR) in sensitivity for detecting pneumothorax, pleural effusion, and interstitial syndrome, making it superior for initial assessment.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: The Bouhemad LUS score (0=normal, 1=B1 lines, 2=B2 lines, 3=consolidation) is the standard semiquantitative tool for monitoring lung re-aeration and predicting response to prone positioning.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: CT remains the gold standard for ARDS morphological phenotyping (focal vs. diffuse), guiding personalized PEEP strategies, as phenotype-mismatched ventilation increases mortality.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: The 'baby lung' concept describes a functionally preserved aerated compartment in non-dependent regions, forming the basis for protective low tidal volume ventilation.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: Electrical impedance tomography (EIT) enables real-time, bedside PEEP titration by detecting regional collapse and overdistension; EIT-guided strategy achieved higher respiratory system compliance (26 vs. 20 mL/cmH2O).

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: CXR has an overall sensitivity of only 40–58% for detecting lung pathology in critically ill patients and should be used selectively for device checks or acute deterioration, not routine daily monitoring.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: EIT is the only bedside tool that can dynamically visualize regional overdistension and pendelluft in real time, though it lacks anatomical resolution and normal reference ranges.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: PET/CT reveals that inflammation in ARDS is diffuse and present even in normally aerated 'baby lung' regions, challenging the notion of spared tissue.

source_paper: Lung Imaging in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: From Physics to Bedside Applications
author: Silvia Coppola et al.
pearl: Dual-energy CT (DECT) can generate iodine distribution maps as a surrogate for regional pulmonary blood volume, potentially revealing occult vasculopathy.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: The 2024 global ARDS definition includes non-intubated patients and no longer requires arterial blood gas analysis, enabling earlier diagnosis in resource-limited settings.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: Transpulmonary thermodilution is the clinical gold standard for measuring pulmonary edema, with EVLWi > 10 mL/kg indicating pulmonary edema and PVPi > 3 confirming hyperpermeability edema.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: Chest CT quantification of ground-glass opacity correlates well with gravimetry or thermodilution in ARDS populations (r = 0.71–0.91), making it a promising non-invasive modality.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: Since pulmonary edema typically peaks by intubation, preventative therapies targeting endothelial barrier function should be administered early, ideally in pre-ICU patients on the ward.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: Hyperpermeability pulmonary edema is characterized by disruption of the endothelial and alveolar epithelial barrier, leading to high protein content fluid (hallmark of ARDS).

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: Hypoxemia in ARDS is primarily caused by a right-to-left shunt (V/Q ratio < 0.01), not diffusion limitation.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: Transpulmonary thermodilution parameters: PVPi < 2 suggests hydrostatic edema, while PVPi > 3 indicates hyperpermeability edema.

source_paper: Measuring and managing pulmonary edema in ARDS: a narrative review
author: Schippers JR et al.
pearl: A subgroup with elevated IL-6, TNFR1, and SP-D responded favorably to imatinib, suggesting personalized therapy potential for pulmonary edema in ARDS.

pearl: Baseline CRP >204 mg·L⁻¹ has been identified as a predictive marker of potential mortality benefit from corticosteroids in severe CAP, but this requires prospective validation before being used as a treatment rule.

source_paper: Systemic corticosteroids for severe community-acquired pneumonia: a narrative review of mechanisms and critical appraisal of recent trials in ICU
author: Benjamin Eastwood et al.
pearl: Early corticosteroid administration (within 24 hours) is associated with greater benefit for sCAP compared to initiation after 48 hours.

source_paper: Systemic corticosteroids for severe community-acquired pneumonia: a narrative review of mechanisms and critical appraisal of recent trials in ICU
author: Benjamin Eastwood et al.
pearl: The heterogeneity of outcomes in large sCAP trials (ESCAPe, CAPE COD, REMAP-CAP) suggests that endotype-based patient selection and precision medicine approaches are necessary.

source_paper: Systemic corticosteroids for severe community-acquired pneumonia: a narrative review of mechanisms and critical appraisal of recent trials in ICU
author: Benjamin Eastwood et al.
pearl: Future sCAP trials should incorporate multi-omics endotyping, rapid molecular pathogen identification, PK-PD monitoring, and follow-up beyond 90 days to enable precision therapy.

source_paper: Systemic corticosteroids for severe community-acquired pneumonia: a narrative review of mechanisms and critical appraisal of recent trials in ICU
author: Benjamin Eastwood et al.
pearl: Corticosteroids possess both genomic (suppressing NF-κβ/AP-1) and nongenomic mechanisms (modulating endothelial NO synthase), suggesting benefit may be confined to specific inflammatory endotypes.

source_paper: Systemic corticosteroids for severe community-acquired pneumonia: a narrative review of mechanisms and critical appraisal of recent trials in ICU
author: Benjamin Eastwood et al.
pearl: The low control group mortality rates in sCAP trials (e.g., REMAP-CAP 9.8%) compared to real-world estimates (~25%) suggest that trial populations may not capture the most severely ill patients.

source_paper: Systemic corticosteroids for severe community-acquired pneumonia: a narrative review of mechanisms and critical appraisal of recent trials in ICU
author: Benjamin Eastwood et al.
pearl: When considering corticosteroid dosing in critical illness, PK-PD monitoring is crucial because only the free fraction binds the glucocorticoid receptor, and fixed dosing ignores inter-individual variability.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: The diagnosis of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis (IPA) relies on a combination of host criteria, clinical/radiological signs, and mycological tests, graded as possible, probable, or proven per EORTC-MSGERC definitions.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Chest CT is the cornerstone of IPA diagnosis; standard chest radiography has poor sensitivity and is not appropriate for screening.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: In haematologic cancer patients, combined serum or BAL galactomannan (GM) and Aspergillus PCR testing achieves high diagnostic accuracy (up to 99% sensitivity and 98% specificity).

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Serum GM sensitivity drops significantly in solid organ transplant recipients (~40%), but BAL testing retains high performance, especially when combining GM and PCR.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Triazoles (voriconazole, isavuconazole, posaconazole) are first-line therapy for proven/probable IPA; liposomal amphotericin B is recommended for azole-resistant IPA or as empirical therapy for possible invasive mould infection.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is recommended for voriconazole (trough 1–5.5 mg/L), posaconazole (prophylaxis >0.7 mg/L, treatment >1.0 mg/L), and itraconazole.

source_paper: Pulmonary aspergillosis: diagnosis and treatment
author: Lamoth F, Calandra T
pearl: Azole-resistant *Aspergillus fumigatus* is associated with significantly higher mortality; novel antifungals like olorofim and fosmanogepix are under investigation for resistant strains.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: For septic shock, initial management requires prompt administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics and vasopressors to maintain a Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) ≥ 65 mmHg.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: If the MAP goal is not met with initial fluids and norepinephrine, consider adding vasopressin or epinephrine as second-line agents.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: The use of continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) in septic shock should be individualized; it is generally reserved for refractory fluid overload, severe acidosis, or acute kidney injury (AKI) that does not respond to standard medical management.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: In patients with suspected septic shock and persistent hypotension despite adequate initial resuscitation, early initiation of vasopressors is recommended rather than waiting for further diagnostic workup.

source_paper: Clinical Application of Electrical Impedance Tomography in Emergency and Critical Care Medicine
author: Yoshiaki Iwashita et al.
pearl: The lactate clearance rate (LCR) should be monitored; a sustained decrease in lactate levels suggests improving tissue perfusion and response to therapy.
